docTitle: To Every Tribe and Tongue · Bible in One Scroll
metaDesc: How the gospel reaches every tongue — rare facts and translation reach gathered while carrying one story into many languages.

s.title: To Every Tribe and Tongue
s.verse: “After this I looked, and behold, a great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages …” (Revelation 7:9)
s.purpose: This page exists for one thing — to show God’s work of carrying the gospel into every tongue. Every number below is not “what we did,” but a prayer offered alongside the task that remains, and thanks for the church that has preserved the Word.
s.f_h: What we met while carrying one story into many tongues
s.f_lead: Quoting Scripture across languages reveals the astonishing diversity God planted in human speech — and the one Christ who runs through all of it.
s.r_lead: By Joshua Project data, the languages carried so far reach about 80% of the world’s people (by mother tongue). The dots not yet filled are the reason for this work.
s.t_lead: There are about 7,400 living languages. Only a fraction have the whole Bible — translation is nearer than ever, yet not finished.
s.t_pray_lead: There are languages we began and then paused. Rather than publish AI prose without a trustworthy native rendering, we chose a faithful “not yet.” Please pray for these — they need human translators.
s.v_kick: ③ The Word holds across tongues
s.v_h: One verse, many scripts — not a single word invented
s.v_lead: Every verse is quoted verbatim from each language’s official Bible translation. The storyline is retold, but the biblical text is never paraphrased. Below is John 3:16.
s.v_note: John 3:16 in {n} scripts — every line verbatim from each language’s official edition. Tap a label ↗ to open that Bible.
s.e_kick: ④ To the ends of the earth
s.e_h: One point, one language — on the earth
s.e_lead: Each carried language is a point where most of its speakers live. Size is mother-tongue speakers; color is how it was carried. Drag to spin.
s.e_hint: Spins on its own · drag to rotate, tap a point to see the language
s.g_h: By continent — where the gospel has reached
s.g_lead: The carried languages, grouped by the continent where most of their speakers live. The bar is how many people they reach in their mother tongue there.
s.m_h: From every nation, tribe, people and language
s.m_lead: Each rectangle is one language. Size is mother-tongue speakers; color is how it was carried — full quotation · New-Testament-centered (partial) · bridged from a neighbor’s Bible · or, where no Bible exists, drawn from an illustrated story-Bible (OBS).
s.i_lead: This is the Great Commission, carried together by the body of Christ. Join in four ways.
s.i_p_b: For languages still without Scripture, and those on the list above — that the Lord would send workers into His harvest.
s.i_s_b: Send someone the page in their own tongue. One link can reach one soul in the language of the heart.
s.i_g_b: Through Bible-translation organizations (Wycliffe and others), so translation can begin for peoples still without the Word.
s.i_t_b: If you are a native speaker of a language, review its prose and help turn a “not yet” into a “now.” No coding needed — just the text.
s.i_cta: See the Bible in One Scroll →
s.foot: The purpose of all this is the proclamation of the gospel — God’s great command. Glory to God alone.
s.world_note: Of about {living} living languages. {need} still await a translation to begin — about {needp} people. The fewest ever, but not zero.
s.why_DEFERRED: Scripture is in hand, but it needs a trustworthy native rendering
s.why_HELD: No fetchable Bible edition found yet
s.why_COVERED-BY-PARENT: Adequately reached by the standard-language page
s.sources: Sources · World Bible-translation figures: {source} ({asof}). Speaker populations: Joshua Project. Biblical text: each language’s official edition (개역개정, ESV, 和合本, Synodal, Van Dyck, and {verx} others).

facts.h: A language with zero native speakers unites a world
facts.p: Almost no one speaks Modern Standard Arabic as a mother tongue — yet from Morocco to Iraq it unites the Arab world in reading. So the site keeps both a standard-Arabic page and several spoken-dialect pages.
Different letters, different sounds — but the one Christ they point to is the same.
facts.multi: 예수 · 耶穌 · يسوع · Iisus · Yesu · యేసు · Jesu · Иисус

facts.p: The psalm of exile is 137 in many Bibles but 136 in the Septuagint/Slavonic tradition (Russian, Georgian, Uzbek…). Each language must be cited in its own Bible’s numbering for the link to land.

facts.h: “Unto us a child is born” — 9:6 or 9:5
facts.p: Isaiah’s famous messianic prophecy is 9:6 in some Bibles (ESV, Synodal) and 9:5 in others (Luther, etc.). We always follow each edition’s own numbering.

facts.h: Some Bibles are just NT + Genesis + Judges
facts.p: Languages without a full Bible aren’t left out — the Kyrgyz edition has only the NT plus Genesis and Judges, so we quote what exists and substitute missing OT verses with NT ones. The living frontier of translation.

facts.h: A doubtful word, checked against its own Bible
facts.p: In siSwati, a few non-standard-looking words were fixed by finding the same word in a real verse of that language’s own Bible. Not guesswork — the Word itself is the standard.

facts.h: German in Brazil, an Indian tongue in Telugu script
facts.p: Hunsrik (a German dialect settled in Brazil), and Lambadi — written in Telugu script though it has no standardized orthography yet. Hundreds of scripts, each with its own challenge.
Verses are quoted verbatim from {VER} official Bible editions. The storyline prose is human-reviewed, but not a single verse of Scripture is ever paraphrased or invented.

facts.h: No Bible yet? Borrow a neighbor’s Word
facts.p: Some languages without their own Bible keep the storyline in the mother tongue but quote verses verbatim from a neighboring language’s official Bible (“bridge” mode) — the very Bible those readers actually use.

facts.h: The only fetchable Cantonese Bible is a 1915 romanized one
facts.p: Most Cantonese readers use Traditional Chinese, yet the only edition we could quote verbatim was a 1915 romanized Bible — still verbatim, so we kept it to give Cantonese a page.
In some editions with deuterocanonical books, similar names can send a link to the wrong book. An automated checker catches such mis-links (e.g. Lamentations vs the Letter of Jeremiah).

facts.h: Chapter and verse, in the language’s own numerals
facts.p: Many languages write chapter:verse in their own numerals (Persian ۱۲۳, Devanagari १२३, and more). We normalize these so every link still resolves correctly.

facts.h: AI never writes a verse from memory
facts.p: Language models tend to plausibly “invent” Bible verses. So every verse is pulled directly from the actual published edition; AI is trusted only for the storyline prose — and even that is reviewed.

facts.h: Same speech, different script, different Bible
facts.p: Azerbaijani has separate Bibles in Latin and Arabic script; Punjabi splits into Gurmukhi and Shahmukhi. The same language can need a separate Bible — and a separate page — for each script tradition.

facts.h: Some Bibles read right to left
facts.p: Arabic, Hebrew, Persian, Urdu and Yiddish flow right to left. For these pages the entire layout — not just the letters — is mirrored.
In the Septuagint tradition (Armenian, Georgian…), Samuel and Kings are counted together as “1–4 Kingdoms.” So the book we call “1 Kings” is the “3rd” there — even book numbers differ by tradition.

facts.h: No Bible yet? An illustrated story-Bible
facts.p: For some peoples with neither a full Bible nor a New Testament, there is an illustrated “Open Bible Stories” (e.g. Balochi). We quote its frames verbatim — a first doorway for the Word to arrive.
